proposals.title: The Causal Asymmetry of Achievement: A Cross-Lagged Panel Analysis of How Prior Math Achievement Predicts Subsequent Motivation, Strategy Use, and Perceived Classroom Demands
proposals.motivation: While the Reciprocal Effects Model (REM) robustly demonstrates that academic self-concept and achievement mutually reinforce each other, the specific directional pathways from a student's achievement level to other critical learning factors remain less explored. Understanding whether high or low math achievement acts as a stronger predictor of subsequent motivational, strategic, and perceptual variables is crucial for designing targeted educational interventions, particularly for students at risk of disengagement.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that prior math achievement will be a stronger predictor of subsequent academic self-concept, learning strategy use, and perceptions of classroom academic demands than these factors are of subsequent achievement, revealing a causal asymmetry that underscores the foundational role of mastery experiences.
proposals.method: We will employ a three-wave longitudinal cross-lagged panel model (CLPM) with a large, representative sample of secondary school students. Using structural equation modeling (SEM) in Mplus, we will test the reciprocal paths between standardized math test scores and latent constructs of motivation (academic self-concept, satisfaction), strategy use (cognitive and metacognitive strategies), and environmental perceptions (perceived academic demands, class climate). Model comparisons will assess the relative strength and significance of the cross-lagged paths.
proposals.contribution: This study will extend the REM by quantifying the directional dominance of achievement over key learning processes, moving beyond the self-concept-achievement dyad. It will provide empirical evidence on whether achievement gains can 'jump-start' positive cycles of motivation and strategy use, informing the timing and focus of instructional support.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in testing the predictive primacy of achievement against a broader set of consequential educational outcomes simultaneously, integrating Marsh's work on self-concept and class effects with contemporary research on achievement emotions and self-regulated learning strategies.

proposals.title: Distinct or Redundant? A Longitudinal MTMM Test of the Discriminant Validity of School Grades and Standardized Test Scores
proposals.motivation: Grades and test scores are used interchangeably as indicators of academic achievement, yet they may reflect distinct psychological constructs with different antecedents and consequences. Pre-2026 research suggests grades are more sensitive to non-cognitive factors like effort and classroom behavior, while tests are purer measures of cognitive ability, but their discriminant validity is often assumed rather than rigorously tested. This conflation hampers educational theory, policy, and practice, particularly in models of academic self-concept and emotion where the referent of evaluation (grades vs. tests) may critically alter effects.
proposals.hypothesis: Latent factors representing school grades and standardized test scores will demonstrate discriminant validity, evidenced by (a) distinct developmental trajectories over time, and (b) differential predictive validity for long-term academic (e.g., university satisfaction) and non-academic (e.g., career entry) outcomes.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-trait (grades, test scores) multi-method (e.g., multiple subjects, multiple test batteries) longitudinal design across three waves (e.g., Grades 9, 11, 12). Data will be analyzed using latent variable structural equation modeling, specifically the Correlated Traits-Correlated Methods (CT-CM) model, to separate trait, method, and error variance. The core test involves comparing a one-trait (general achievement) model against a two-trait (grades, tests) model using chi-square difference tests and indices like CFI and RMSEA. We will then model latent growth curves for each factor and use them in a RI-CLPM to predict distal outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the most rigorous, falsifiable evidence to date on whether grades and test scores are functionally distinct. It will directly inform theoretical models of achievement, self-concept, and emotion by clarifying the specific achievement referent. Findings will have practical implications for educational assessment, accountability, and interventions targeting academic development and well-being.
proposals.novelty: While MTMM designs have been used cross-sectionally, applying them longitudinally to model developmental trajectories and long-term predictive validity of the grade and test factors is novel. This moves beyond simple correlation to test dynamic, process-oriented discriminant validity, a key advancement for Marsh's Reciprocal Effects Model and research on achievement emotions.

proposals.title: Testing the Developmental Equilibrium of the Reciprocal Effects Model: A Longitudinal Study from Adolescence to Early Adulthood
proposals.motivation: The Reciprocal Effects Model (REM), a cornerstone of Herbert Marsh's work, robustly demonstrates that prior academic self-concept and achievement reciprocally influence each other. However, its empirical validation is largely confined to school-aged samples, primarily up to Grade 9. It is unknown whether this dynamic equilibrium persists as individuals transition through later adolescence and into adulthood, where educational and career pathways diversify and self-concept may stabilize or become more trait-like. Testing this assumption is critical for understanding the lifespan applicability of the REM and its implications for interventions beyond compulsory schooling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the reciprocal cross-lagged effects between academic self-concept and achievement will weaken in magnitude from late adolescence (Grade 10/11) into early adulthood (post-secondary education/early career), as domain-specific self-concepts become more crystallized and external influences (e.g., job demands, life satisfaction) grow in importance.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-cohort longitudinal design, tracking students from Grade 9 through to approximately age 25. Data will include standardized achievement test scores and self-reported academic self-concept, supplemented by pre-2025 measures of academic demands, time use, and satisfaction. The core analysis will use Random Intercept Cross-Lagged Panel Models (RI-CLPM), which separate within-person dynamics from stable between-person differences. Model comparisons will test for invariance in cross-lagged and autoregressive parameters across developmental stages (e.g., high school vs. university vs. workforce).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first rigorous test of the REM's boundary conditions across a critical developmental transition. It will clarify whether the model's core reciprocal mechanism is a fundamental feature of human development or a phenomenon specific to structured educational environments. The findings will inform the design of support programs for older adolescents and young adults, shifting focus if self-concept becomes less malleable to achievement feedback.
proposals.novelty: While the REM is well-established for school-aged youth, no study has systematically examined whether its reciprocal dynamics hold—or decay—as individuals exit compulsory schooling and face the more heterogeneous demands of adulthood, a key untested assumption of the model's generalizability.

proposals.title: Mapping the Dynamic Network Structure of Academic Self-Concept and Achievement: A Longitudinal Network Psychometrics Approach
proposals.motivation: The Reciprocal Effects Model (REM) robustly establishes a positive feedback loop between global academic self-concept and achievement. However, pre-2026 research largely treats these constructs as monolithic, obscuring the dynamic interplay between specific self-beliefs (e.g., math self-concept) and corresponding skills. Network psychometrics offers a novel framework to model these constructs as systems of interacting components, which is crucial for developing targeted interventions that address specific, problematic belief-skill linkages rather than global traits.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that within the broader REM, the network of specific self-concepts and achievement domains will show strong, reciprocal within-domain connections (e.g., math self-concept and math grade) and weaker cross-domain connections, and that the strength and direction of these edges will predict subsequent changes in student emotions (e.g., academic anxiety) and overall academic satisfaction.
proposals.method: We will apply longitudinal network analysis to existing panel data (e.g., from the Marsh Self-Description Questionnaires and standardized test scores). Using the R package `mgm` or `graphicalVAR`, we will estimate temporal and contemporaneous networks to identify central nodes and directional influences among specific self-concept facets and achievement measures. Network comparison tests will be used to analyze structural differences across key groups, such as high- versus low-achieving classes, addressing Marsh's recent findings on class-average effects.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first fine-grained, dynamic network map of the REM, moving beyond latent variable models to identify specific, actionable pathways in the self-concept-achievement system. It will directly inform more precise intervention strategies in educational psychology by pinpointing critical leverage points (e.g., specific self-beliefs) that most strongly influence the network. The integration with emotion regulation strategies, a recent focus of Marsh's work, will bridge cognitive and affective educational models.
proposals.novelty: While Marsh's work has definitively validated the REM using latent variable models, applying network psychometrics to decompose these constructs and model their dynamic interactions at a granular level is a novel and non-obvious extension. This approach tests whether the REM's reciprocity is driven by a few strong, specific pathways rather than uniform effects across all domains.

proposals.title: Testing the Developmental Stability of the Reciprocal Effects Model: A Micro-Intervention Study from Adolescence to Adulthood
proposals.motivation: The Reciprocal Effects Model (REM) robustly demonstrates a positive feedback loop between academic self-concept and achievement in school-aged youth. However, it is unknown whether this dynamic equilibrium becomes more entrenched and resistant to change as individuals move into adulthood, where educational structures and social comparisons shift. This gap limits our understanding of the lifespan malleability of self-beliefs and their causal role in performance beyond compulsory schooling.
proposals.hypothesis: The strength of the reciprocal relationship between academic self-concept and achievement, and its susceptibility to exogenous perturbation, will diminish from late adolescence into adulthood, indicating increased developmental equilibrium.
proposals.method: We will employ a longitudinal, multi-cohort micro-intervention design. Participants (ages 16-30) will be randomly assigned to a brief, validated online self-concept boost (e.g., reflecting on past successes) or a neutral control at Time 1. Using latent change score modeling within a structural equation framework, we will analyze immediate (T1), short-term (1-week, T2), and longer-term (6-month, T3) effects on domain-specific self-concept (questionnaire) and objective achievement (standardized test or GPA). The design explicitly tests for age and cohort as moderators of the intervention's effect size and its persistence over time.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first causal, lifespan test of the REM's boundary conditions, revealing if and when the self-concept-achievement loop becomes fixed. It will advance theory on developmental equilibrium and inform the optimal timing for psychological interventions aimed at breaking negative feedback cycles in education and adult learning.
proposals.novelty: While the REM is well-established, applying precise micro-interventions to probe its stability across developmental stages beyond adolescence is a novel, causal approach that moves beyond correlational longitudinal observation.

proposals.title: Micro-Interventions for Academic Self-Concept: A Randomized Field Experiment Testing Immediate Effects on Achievement Emotions and Performance
proposals.motivation: The Reciprocal Effects Model (REM) robustly demonstrates a long-term, mutually reinforcing relationship between academic self-concept and achievement. However, the field lacks evidence on whether brief, targeted psychological interventions can trigger this positive cycle in the short term within authentic classroom settings. Testing such micro-interventions addresses a critical gap in translating longitudinal theory into practical, scalable tools for teachers to enhance student emotions and learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a brief, in-class self-concept micro-intervention (e.g., a values-affirmation or best-possible-self writing exercise) will produce immediate, measurable improvements in students' state academic self-concept and positive achievement emotions (e.g., enjoyment), which will in turn lead to enhanced performance on a subsequent academic task within the same instructional session.
proposals.method: We will conduct a randomized controlled trial in secondary school mathematics classes. Students will be randomly assigned to a treatment group (completing a 10-minute self-concept micro-intervention) or an active control group. Immediately before and after the intervention, students will complete ultra-brief state measures of academic self-concept and achievement emotions (e.g., the Achievement Emotions Questionnaire–Short Form). All students will then complete a standardized, curriculum-based mathematics test. Data will be analyzed using a 2 (condition) x 2 (time) mixed ANOVA for the psychological outcomes and an ANCOVA (controlling for prior achievement) for the test performance, with mediation analysis testing the proposed pathway.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first experimental evidence within Marsh's REM framework on the causal efficacy of brief, in-situ self-concept interventions. It will directly inform educational practice by identifying a low-cost, feasible strategy for teachers to boost student motivation and learning. The findings will also refine theoretical understanding of the temporal dynamics and mechanisms linking self-concept, emotions, and achievement.
proposals.novelty: While REM research is extensive, it has not isolated the immediate, causal effects of a psychological micro-intervention on both self-concept/emotions and a proximal achievement outcome within a single class period. This moves beyond correlational panel models and long-term interventions to test the theory's mechanistic assumptions at a granular, actionable timescale.

proposals.title: The Dual Mediation of Teacher Expectations: A Cross-Lagged Panel Model of Their Effects on Student Achievement and Academic Self-Concept
proposals.motivation: Teacher expectations are a well-documented influence on student outcomes, but their role is often studied in isolation. Pre-2026 research by Marsh and others highlights the complex, reciprocal relationships between academic self-concept and achievement. This proposal addresses a critical gap by investigating how teacher perceptions simultaneously mediate and are influenced by both constructs, moving beyond unidirectional models to understand the dual-problem nature of expectation effects within the established reciprocal effects framework.
proposals.hypothesis: Teacher expectations will act as a significant dual mediator in the reciprocal relationship between academic achievement and academic self-concept, such that prior achievement and self-concept predict subsequent teacher expectations, which in turn predict subsequent levels of both achievement and self-concept.
proposals.method: We will employ a three-wave longitudinal cross-lagged panel model (CLPM) with structured latent variables, analyzing data from a large-scale educational panel study (e.g., German National Educational Panel Study, NEPS). The core analysis will be a dual mediation CLPM where paths from Time 1 (T1) achievement and self-concept to Time 2 (T2) teacher expectations, and from T2 expectations to Time 3 (T3) achievement and self-concept, are estimated simultaneously, controlling for autoregressive effects. Model fit will be assessed using pre-2026 standards (CFI > .95, RMSEA < .06, SRMR < .08) via Mplus 8.0, with robust maximum likelihood estimation.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a more integrated, dynamic account of how teacher expectations function within the academic self-system, directly testing their dual mediating role. It will extend the Reciprocal Effects Model by formally incorporating a key social-contextual factor as both an outcome and a driver of student development, offering practical insights for teacher training focused on mitigating biased expectation effects.
proposals.novelty: While teacher expectations and the reciprocal effects model are established, explicitly modeling expectations as a shared mediator in the bidirectional relationship between self-concept and achievement represents a novel integration. This moves beyond treating expectations as a simple predictor or outcome, framing them as a core component of the dynamic feedback loop that shapes academic development.

proposals.title: Teacher Enthusiasm as a Buffer: A Cross-Level Interaction Effect on Academic Self-Concept and Emotions for Low-Ability Students in High-Achieving Classes
proposals.motivation: Research on the Big-Fish-Little-Pond Effect (BFLPE) robustly shows that attending high-achieving classes can depress students' academic self-concept. However, pre-2026 work has largely treated classroom climate as monolithic, overlooking how specific, malleable teacher behaviors might moderate this negative social comparison effect. This is critical for developing targeted interventions to support vulnerable students within high-achieving educational contexts.
proposals.hypothesis: Teacher enthusiasm interacts with individual student ability and class-average achievement, such that its positive effect on academic self-concept and positive achievement emotions is strongest for low-ability students within high-achieving classes, thereby buffering the typical BFLPE for this subgroup.
proposals.method: We will employ a longitudinal, multilevel structural equation modeling (ML-SEM) design with three waves. Using pre-existing large-scale datasets (e.g., PISA, TIMSS, or national panel studies), we will model a cross-level interaction. At the student level (Level 1), we include prior ability, academic self-concept, and achievement emotions. At the class level (Level 2), we include class-average achievement and a latent factor for teacher enthusiasm (measured by student and/or observer reports). The key test is the cross-level interaction between class-level enthusiasm and student-level ability on changes in self-concept and emotions, controlling for prior scores.
proposals.contribution: This study will extend the BFLPE and Reciprocal Effects Model literatures by identifying a specific, observable teacher characteristic that can mitigate negative peer comparison effects. It will provide a more nuanced, person-environment interaction perspective on classroom effects, offering evidence-based guidance for teacher professional development focused on equitable support in high-track settings.
proposals.novelty: While prior research has established the main effects of teacher enthusiasm and the BFLPE separately, the proposed three-way interaction (enthusiasm × individual ability × class achievement) is novel. It challenges the deterministic view of the BFLPE by testing a moderating mechanism that is directly actionable for educators.

proposals.title: Personality as a Buffer: Testing Resilience and Competitiveness as Moderators of the Big-Fish-Little-Pond Effect on Academic Self-Concept
proposals.motivation: The Big-Fish-Little-Pond Effect (BFLPE), where students in high-achieving reference groups develop lower academic self-concepts, is a robust but demotivating phenomenon. Pre-2026 research has focused on contextual moderators, with limited investigation of individual-difference factors that might mitigate this negative effect. Identifying such personal buffers is crucial for developing targeted interventions to protect student self-perception and motivation in competitive academic settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that student personality traits, specifically higher resilience and higher competitiveness, will significantly attenuate the negative impact of class-average achievement on academic self-concept, serving as protective moderators of the BFLPE.
proposals.method: We will employ a large-scale, longitudinal panel design with secondary school students, measuring academic self-concept, achievement, class-average achievement, and personality traits (using established scales for resilience and competitiveness) across multiple time points. The core analysis will use latent moderated structural equation modeling (LMS) within an autoregressive cross-lagged panel model framework. This allows for a rigorous test of the interaction effects between the latent trait variables and class-average achievement on subsequent academic self-concept, while controlling for prior levels and reciprocal effects.
proposals.contribution: This study will extend the BFLPE literature by moving beyond demographic and contextual moderators to establish a key role for malleable psychological traits. It will provide empirical evidence for which students are most vulnerable or protected in high-ability settings, informing more nuanced theories of social comparison in education. The findings will offer practical insights for designing socio-emotional learning programs aimed at fostering resilience to counteract negative peer comparisons.
proposals.novelty: While the BFLPE is well-established, its interaction with specific, theoretically relevant personality constructs like resilience and competitiveness remains largely untested. This integrates Marsh's work on academic self-concept with contemporary personality and motivation research to explain variance in a core educational psychology model.

proposals.title: Modeling the Social Contagion of Academic Self-Concept: A Longitudinal Social Network Analysis of Peer Influence in Secondary School Classrooms
proposals.motivation: Prior research on the Big-Fish-Little-Pond Effect (BFLPE) and class-average achievement has established contextual effects on academic self-concept (ASC), but it largely treats the classroom as a monolithic context. This overlooks the nuanced, dyadic pathways of peer influence within friendship networks, which are critical for understanding how ASC and achievement are socially transmitted and maintained. A network-informed approach is needed to move beyond aggregate models and directly test mechanisms of social comparison and influence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that students' academic self-concept and achievement are influenced not only by the overall classroom average but also by the ASC and achievement of their direct friends and their friends' friends (indirect influence), with these network effects being stronger for ASC than for achievement itself.
proposals.method: We will collect longitudinal data (three waves across one academic year) from approximately 1500 secondary school students in 60 classes, using sociometric nominations to map complete classroom friendship networks. We will employ Stochastic Actor-Oriented Models (SAOMs) as implemented in RSiena to disentangle selection (students choosing friends with similar ASC) from influence (friends affecting a student's ASC). The analysis will control for key individual and classroom-level covariates, including prior achievement and class-average achievement, to isolate network effects.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, longitudinal test of peer influence on ASC using dynamic network modeling, moving beyond correlational and contextual-effect designs. It will quantify the relative strength of direct versus indirect peer influence and clarify whether social networks amplify or mitigate the classic BFLPE, offering a more precise social mechanism for the Reciprocal Effects Model.
proposals.novelty: While Marsh's prior work robustly establishes the BFLPE, this proposal innovatively integrates social network analysis (SNA) and SAOMs—methods not yet combined in his published ASC research—to model the micro-social processes underlying these well-established macro-level effects.

proposals.title: The Big-Fish-Little-Pond Effect at Scale: Disentangling the Unique Effects of School-Level and Neighborhood Achievement Contexts on Student Self-Concept and Emotions
proposals.motivation: The Big-Fish-Little-Pond Effect (BFLPE) robustly shows that class-average achievement negatively impacts academic self-concept. However, recent pre-2026 findings indicate that attending a high-achieving class is not beneficial for achievement itself, raising critical questions about the broader ecological context. It remains unknown whether the achievement climate of the entire school or the surrounding neighborhood exerts distinct psychological effects beyond the immediate classroom, which is vital for understanding the full scope of contextual influences on student development.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that school-wide and neighborhood-level achievement averages will have significant negative effects on academic self-concept and positive effects on negative achievement emotions (e.g., anxiety), even after controlling for the well-established classroom-level BFLPE.
proposals.method: We will employ multilevel structural equation modeling (ML-SEM) with cross-classified random effects to disentangle variance attributable to classroom, school, and neighborhood contexts. Using large-scale longitudinal data (e.g., from PISA or national panel studies with geocoded student addresses), we will model the reciprocal relationships between achievement, academic self-concept, and achievement emotions over time, incorporating school-average and neighborhood-average achievement (based on census or administrative data) as distinct Level-2 predictors. The analysis will control for individual socioeconomic status and prior achievement.
proposals.contribution: This study will extend the BFLPE and Reciprocal Effects Model literature by formally testing the unique predictive power of larger ecological units. It will provide crucial evidence for policymakers and educators on whether school tracking, zoning policies, or school-wide branding based on achievement aggregates have unintended psychological consequences for students.
proposals.novelty: While the classroom-level BFLPE is well-documented, systematically partitioning the variance in self-concept and emotions between classroom, school, and neighborhood contexts is a novel and non-obvious extension, moving beyond the immediate instructional setting to the broader social frame of reference.

proposals.title: Optimizing Class Composition: A Computational Inverse Modeling Approach to Maximize Achievement and Academic Self-Concept
proposals.motivation: Educational policy and school tracking decisions often rely on assumptions about the benefits of homogeneous ability grouping, yet Marsh's pre-2026 research shows attending high-achieving classes can be neutral or detrimental for individual student outcomes. There is a critical need to move beyond descriptive models of the Big-Fish-Little-Pond Effect (BFLPE) to prescriptive tools that can inform optimal class assignment, balancing aggregate achievement with positive academic self-concept—a key motivational construct.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an optimal, non-homogeneous distribution of student abilities within a class exists, which maximizes a multi-objective function combining average achievement and aggregate academic self-concept, and that this optimum can be identified via computational inverse modeling of established reciprocal effects models.
proposals.method: We will employ an inverse modeling framework using established longitudinal panel data (e.g., from the German National Educational Panel Study). First, we will specify and validate a structural equation model (SEM) integrating the Reciprocal Effects Model (achievement <-> self-concept) and the BFLPE. Second, we will treat this validated SEM as a forward model within an optimization loop. Using a genetic algorithm, we will iteratively simulate different hypothetical class compositions (distributions of prior achievement) to find the input distribution that maximizes a defined outcome function (e.g., weighted sum of end-of-year achievement and self-concept scores). Sensitivity analyses will test robustness across different weighting schemes and school contexts.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first computational, optimization-based method for deriving ideal class compositions from well-established psychological models. It will shift the discourse from describing the effects of existing groupings to proactively designing them. The results will offer empirically grounded, non-intuitive guidelines for school administrators seeking to improve both cognitive and affective student outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While Marsh's work has robustly modeled the *effects* of class composition, this proposal inverts the paradigm to *solve for* an optimal composition—a novel application of inverse problem-solving from engineering to educational psychology. It moves beyond testing null effects of high-achieving classes to actively searching for a positive alternative.

proposals.title: Reciprocal Effects of Academic Self-Concept and Test Anxiety: A Longitudinal Cross-Lagged Panel Analysis in Secondary School Students
proposals.motivation: Prior research on the Reciprocal Effects Model (REM) has robustly established the mutual reinforcement between academic self-concept and achievement. However, its application to critical non-academic outcomes, particularly test anxiety, remains underexplored. Understanding the bidirectional relationship between self-concept and anxiety is crucial for developing interventions that simultaneously improve students' psychological well-being and academic functioning, a key pre-2026 concern in educational psychology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a negative reciprocal relationship exists between academic self-concept and test anxiety over time, where lower self-concept predicts subsequent increases in anxiety, and higher anxiety predicts subsequent declines in self-concept. We further hypothesize that this reciprocal relationship will be robust after controlling for prior achievement.
proposals.method: We will employ a three-wave longitudinal design (Time 1, 2, 3 over one academic year) with a large, representative sample of secondary school students (N ≈ 2000). Using structural equation modeling (SEM), we will specify a cross-lagged panel model (CLPM) with latent variables. The core model will include the autoregressive paths for self-concept and anxiety and the cross-lagged paths between them. We will control for the stability of academic achievement (standardized test scores) by including it as a time-varying covariate. Model fit will be assessed using CFI, TLI, RMSEA, and SRMR, with robust maximum likelihood estimation.
proposals.contribution: This study will extend the REM beyond achievement to a critical affective domain, providing empirical evidence on the dynamic interplay between self-perception and emotional distress. It will inform targeted intervention strategies by identifying whether academic self-concept or test anxiety is a more potent leading indicator for change, contributing to more holistic student support frameworks.
proposals.novelty: While the REM is well-established for achievement, applying its rigorous longitudinal modeling framework to test anxiety is novel. It moves beyond examining simple correlations to test directional, reciprocal causality between a core self-belief and a debilitating emotion, addressing a significant gap in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: The Long Shadow of the Big-Fish-Little-Pond: Longitudinal Effects of Academic Self-Concept and Peer Spillover on University Choices and Early Career Outcomes
proposals.motivation: The Big-Fish-Little-Pond Effect (BFLPE) and peer achievement spillovers are well-documented influences on adolescent academic self-concept and concurrent achievement. However, their long-term consequences for pivotal life decisions, such as university major selection and early career entry, remain largely unexplored. Understanding these pathways is crucial for evaluating the lasting impact of school tracking and ability grouping on educational inequality and career development.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the negative BFLPE on academic self-concept in high school will predict less ambitious university choices (e.g., lower-prestige institutions, less demanding majors) and lower-status early career outcomes, even after controlling for individual achievement. Conversely, positive peer spillover effects on achievement will show independent, positive long-term effects on these outcomes.
proposals.method: We will analyze longitudinal panel data (e.g., from the German National Educational Panel Study, NEPS) tracking students from secondary school into university and the labor market. Using a Random Intercept Cross-Lagged Panel Model (RI-CLPM), we will separate stable between-person differences from within-person processes. Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) with latent variables will test the direct and mediated pathways from Grade 10 class-average achievement (the "pond") and individual achievement to later outcomes, with academic self-concept and achievement emotions as key mediators. Models will control for socioeconomic status, gender, and prior achievement.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive test of the long-term educational and career consequences of the BFLPE and peer spillover, moving beyond short-term academic outcomes. It will clarify whether the BFLPE's impact is transient or casts a 'long shadow' on life trajectories. The findings will inform policy debates on school structures and counseling practices aimed at mitigating negative long-term effects of social comparisons.
proposals.novelty: While the BFLPE is robust for concurrent self-concept, its longitudinal predictive power for concrete post-secondary decisions is untested. This proposal uniquely integrates the BFLPE framework with life-course sociology to examine how school-based social comparisons crystallize into lasting disparities, a significant gap in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Beyond the Classroom: A Multilevel Investigation of School-Level Big-Fish-Little-Pond Effects and Peer Spillover on Institutional Climate and Outcomes
proposals.motivation: The Big-Fish-Little-Pond Effect (BFLPE) and peer spillover are well-established at the individual and class levels, influencing student self-concept and achievement. However, educational policy and accountability often operate at the school or district level, creating a critical gap in understanding whether these comparative and contextual processes aggregate to shape whole-school climates and institutional performance metrics. Investigating this scale-up is essential for evaluating system-level interventions and understanding the broader institutional consequences of student body composition.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that attending a higher-achieving school, net of individual ability, will have a negative effect on school-level aggregates of academic self-concept and satisfaction (a school-level BFLPE) and that school-level average achievement will positively predict school-level outcomes like climate and well-being through peer spillover processes.
proposals.method: We will employ multilevel structural equation modeling (ML-SEM) with cross-lagged panels on large-scale longitudinal data (e.g., PISA, TIMSS, or state-level administrative datasets). The analysis will specify a 2-3 level model (students nested within schools, within districts/time) to partition variance and test cross-level effects. Key analyses will include contextual effect models and random intercept cross-lagged panel models (RI-CLPM) to examine reciprocal relationships between school-average achievement, school-aggregated self-concept/emotions, and school-level climate/outcome indicators over time.
proposals.contribution: This study will extend the BFLPE and peer influence literature beyond the individual to the institutional level, providing evidence for or against the 'school-level effect' assumption in policy. It will offer a novel framework for analyzing how student body composition impacts school climate reports and aggregate outcomes, informing more nuanced school accountability and improvement strategies.
proposals.novelty: While Marsh's work has robustly examined class-average effects, explicitly testing if these social comparison and peer processes manifest and interact at the broader school level—affecting institutional rather than just individual outcomes—represents a significant and untested scaling of the theoretical model.

proposals.title: Testing the Reciprocal Effects Model in Physical Domains: A Longitudinal Study of Physical Self-Concept and Athletic Performance
proposals.motivation: Herbert Marsh's Reciprocal Effects Model (REM) is a cornerstone of educational psychology, robustly demonstrating that academic self-concept and achievement mutually reinforce each other over time. However, its generalizability to non-academic domains remains underexplored. Extending this framework to physical self-concept and athletic performance is crucial for validating the REM as a universal motivational theory and for informing interventions in sports and physical education, where self-perceptions are critical for participation and success.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that physical self-concept and objectively measured athletic performance will exhibit reciprocal positive effects over time, consistent with the REM. Furthermore, we hypothesize that the strength of these reciprocal effects will be moderated by factors such as gender and prior sport involvement.
proposals.method: We will employ a three-wave longitudinal panel design over one academic year, sampling adolescents from diverse schools. Physical self-concept will be measured using the well-validated Physical Self-Description Questionnaire (PSDQ). Athletic performance will be assessed via standardized objective measures (e.g., 20m shuttle run, standing broad jump). Data analysis will use structural equation modeling (SEM), specifically cross-lagged panel models (CLPM) with latent variables, to test for reciprocal effects. We will compare nested models (e.g., stability, forward, reciprocal) using robust maximum likelihood estimation and fit indices (CFI, RMSEA, SRMR), following Marsh's established protocols for academic domains.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first rigorous, longitudinal test of the REM in a physical domain, potentially establishing it as a general model of self-concept development. It will offer concrete evidence for sports psychologists and educators on how to structure feedback and training to enhance both physical self-perceptions and performance. The findings will bridge educational and sport psychology literatures.
proposals.novelty: While the REM is well-established academically, its application to physical/athletic contexts with objective performance measures is novel. This moves beyond correlational studies to test causal dynamics, and investigates domain-specific moderators not examined in academic settings, challenging the assumption that the REM's mechanisms are identical across all domains.

proposals.title: The Antecedents of Emotion Regulation Strategy Selection: A Longitudinal Test of Prior Well-being and Achievement as Determinants
proposals.motivation: Research on achievement emotion regulation, including Marsh's recent work, predominantly examines how strategies influence subsequent emotions and outcomes. This assumes strategy selection is a starting point. However, pre-2026 theories of self-regulation and the Reciprocal Effects Model (REM) suggest that personal resources and prior self-concept shape engagement in adaptive behaviors. It is crucial to test if the choice of regulation strategies is itself an outcome, determined by students' pre-existing academic and emotional baselines.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that higher prior levels of academic self-concept and subjective well-being predict greater subsequent use of adaptive emotion regulation strategies (e.g., cognitive reappraisal), while lower prior achievement and well-being predict greater use of maladaptive strategies (e.g., suppression).
proposals.method: We will employ a three-wave longitudinal panel design over one academic year with university students (N ≈ 1200). Measures will include academic achievement (grades), academic self-concept (ASC), subjective well-being (PANAS, life satisfaction), and the Achievement Emotion Regulation Questionnaire (AERQ). Analysis will use Random Intercept Cross-Lagged Panel Models (RI-CLPM) in Mplus to disentangle within-person processes from stable between-person differences. This allows us to test if within-person fluctuations in prior well-being/achievement predict subsequent within-person changes in strategy use, controlling for autoregressive and reciprocal effects.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel test of the REM by applying it to the domain of emotion regulation, clarifying the directional precedence between personal resources and regulatory action. It will inform targeted interventions by identifying which students are less likely to select adaptive strategies autonomously, thus needing more scaffolded support.
proposals.novelty: While strategy effectiveness is well-studied, systematically inverting the causal direction to treat strategy selection as a dependent variable is novel. It challenges the implicit assumption in intervention research that all students are equally positioned to choose and apply recommended strategies.

proposals.title: A Longitudinal Intervention Study: Teaching RAEQ-Based Emotion Regulation Strategies to Improve Academic Self-Concept and Achievement in Secondary School Students
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Marsh's own work, establishes that achievement emotions are critical for student well-being and academic success. However, the causal efficacy of teaching specific, theory-based emotion regulation strategies from instruments like the Regulation of Achievement Emotions Questionnaire (RAEQ) remains under-tested in real-world school settings. An intervention directly targeting these regulatory skills is needed to move beyond correlational findings and provide actionable tools for educators.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a targeted psychoeducational intervention teaching adaptive emotion regulation strategies (as operationalized by the RAEQ) will lead to significant improvements in students' academic self-concept, academic achievement, and overall school satisfaction, compared to a control group receiving standard pastoral care.
proposals.method: We will conduct a cluster-randomized controlled trial with ~40 secondary school classes. The intervention group will receive a manualized, multi-session program delivered by trained teachers, focusing on RAEQ strategies like cognitive reappraisal and refocusing on planning. Using structural equation modeling (SEM), we will analyze pre-, post-, and follow-up data (achievement tests, Academic Self-Description Questionnaire, RAEQ, satisfaction scales) to test a cross-lagged panel model. This will assess the reciprocal effects between taught regulation strategies, emotions, self-concept, and achievement over time.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, causal evidence on the educational benefits of teaching structured emotion regulation. It will directly extend the Reciprocal Effects Model by integrating a malleable regulatory component, offering a practical, evidence-based intervention module for schools. The findings will bridge the literature on academic self-concept, achievement emotions, and self-regulated learning.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has documented correlations between emotion regulation and outcomes, this study innovates by actively teaching the specific strategies from the RAEQ framework in a school-based RCT and testing their causal impact on the well-established constructs of academic self-concept and achievement within Marsh's reciprocal effects paradigm.

proposals.title: Cultural Variation in the Use and Efficacy of Achievement Emotion Regulation Strategies: A Multi-Group Cross-Lagged Panel Study
proposals.motivation: Research on achievement emotions and their regulation is burgeoning, yet it remains largely Western-centric. Pre-2026 work by Pekrun, Marsh, and others highlights the critical role of emotions in academic outcomes, but the cultural generalizability of findings on regulation strategies (e.g., cognitive reappraisal, suppression) is unclear. Understanding how cultural contexts shape the selection and effectiveness of these strategies is vital for developing universally applicable and culturally sensitive educational interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that (a) the prevalence of specific emotion regulation strategies (e.g., cognitive reappraisal vs. expressive suppression) will vary systematically across cultural groups characterized by different values (e.g., individualism-collectivism), and (b) the longitudinal effect of strategy use on subsequent academic satisfaction and achievement will be moderated by these cultural dimensions.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-group, multi-wave cross-lagged panel model (CLPM) within a structural equation modeling (SEM) framework. Data will be collected via surveys from university students in at least six countries (e.g., Australia, Germany, China, Japan, USA, Mexico) at two time points. The model will include latent variables for regulation strategies (using established scales), achievement emotions, academic self-concept, and objective grades. Multi-group invariance testing and moderated mediation analyses will be conducted to test for cultural moderation of the strategy→emotion→outcome pathways.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, longitudinal test of cultural moderation in the Reciprocal Effects Model (REM) framework applied to emotion regulation. It will advance theory by delineating boundary conditions for emotion regulation effectiveness and offer practical insights for tailoring student well-being programs to diverse cultural and educational settings.
proposals.novelty: While prior cross-cultural psychology has compared emotion regulation, this study uniquely integrates it with Marsh's REM and achievement emotion frameworks to test culturally moderated longitudinal effects on core academic outcomes, moving beyond simple mean-level comparisons.

ideas.title: Micro-Interventions to Probe and Perturb Developmental Equilibrium in Adolescence and Adulthood
ideas.description: This research program combines the precision of micro-interventions with the longitudinal scope of testing developmental equilibrium beyond early adolescence. It involves administering brief, targeted self-concept boosts to individuals across a wide age range (from late adolescence into adulthood) and measuring the immediate, short-term, and longer-term effects on specific achievement metrics. By applying these micro-perturbations at different developmental stages, the design can directly test whether the assumed equilibrium between self-concept and achievement becomes more resistant to change with age or shifts in its responsiveness. This allows for a dynamic, causal mapping of how the pathways between self-belief and performance evolve across the lifespan.

ideas.title: Dynamic Measurement Fallacies in Developmental Pathways
ideas.description: This research direction posits that many documented jingle-jangle fallacies—where different concepts share a name (jingle) or the same concept has different names (jangle)—may be artifacts of measuring constructs within time-invariant models when the underlying reciprocal pathways are actually strength-varying and phase-dependent. By modeling how the strength and direction of relationships between constructs (e.g., self-esteem and academic achievement) shift at key developmental transitions, we can test if a 'fallacy' disappears when measurements are aligned to the correct temporal dynamics of the system. This moves beyond asking if a fallacy is a measurement error to asking *when* a measurement is valid, fundamentally challenging the universality of psychological constructs by temporal context.

ideas.title: Dynamic Causal Loop: How Self-Beliefs Drive and Are Rewritten by Social Comparisons and Their Outcomes
ideas.description: This research synthesizes two causal directions into a single dynamic model. It investigates how initial self-beliefs (e.g., self-concept, self-efficacy) causally determine which social comparisons a student selects (upward, downward, lateral). The model then incorporates how the sustained outcomes of those chosen comparisons (success or failure feedback loops) retroactively reshape and potentially redefine the very self-belief constructs that initiated the selection. This creates a testable framework for understanding virtuous or vicious cycles in academic development, where the distinction between self-belief constructs may blur or solidify based on the historical path of comparison outcomes they have generated.

ideas.description: This research program rigorously tests whether grades and standardized test scores represent psychologically and functionally distinct constructs or are merely method-bound expressions of a general achievement factor. It uses a multi-trait multi-method longitudinal design, collecting multiple grade and test indicators over time to model latent factors while controlling for measurement error and method effects. The core test is whether the two latent factors demonstrate discriminant validity through differential developmental trajectories and uniquely predict distinct long-term academic and career outcomes, providing falsifiable evidence for or against their conceptual separation.

ideas.title: A Longitudinal and Multi-Cohort Investigation of the Dimensionality and Nomological Network of Self-Regulation from Childhood to Early Adulthood
ideas.description: This research precisely defines the target construct as self-regulation and its hypothesized subcomponents (e.g., cognitive, emotional, behavioral control). It employs a multi-cohort sequential longitudinal design, tracking distinct age cohorts (e.g., grades 2, 6, 10, and university freshmen) over three years with annual assessments. The system uses a multi-method battery (performance tasks, teacher/parent reports, self-reports, experience sampling) to establish measurement invariance across ages and to map how the internal structure (dimensionality) and external correlates (nomological network) of self-regulation evolve developmentally, rigorously distinguishing true age-related change from cohort-specific effects.

ideas.title: Developmental Trajectories of Psychological Constructs: A Cross-Sectional Cohort Design
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from developmental biology—specifically, the use of cross-sectional cohort sampling and landmark-based geometric morphometrics to map morphological change—to the psychological question of how distinctions between constructs vary from elementary school to university. In the analogous field, biologists sample organisms at different life stages to quantify shape trajectories and identify phases of allometric growth. The adapted idea applies this logic by treating psychological constructs as shapes in a latent space. A study would collect multi-construct measurement data (e.g., via surveys or cognitive tests) from distinct age/educational cohorts (elementary, middle, high school, university). Advanced multivariate techniques (e.g., growth mixture modeling, multi-group confirmatory factor analysis, or discriminant analysis) would then be used to map the 'developmental trajectories' of the constructs. The goal is to determine whether the distinctions between constructs (their discriminant validity) remain stable, blur (converge), or sharpen (diverge) across educational stages, thereby revealing the allometry of psychological development.

ideas.title: Theory-Driven Bifactor Modeling: Testing the Causal and Functional Distinctness of General and Specific Self-Construct Factors
ideas.description: This research program moves beyond purely statistical model comparison to test a substantive theory about the origins and functions of general versus domain-specific self-constructs. It employs a longitudinal experimental design where self-concept and self-efficacy are measured across multiple domains before and after targeted interventions that manipulate experiences in specific domains (e.g., academic, social). The core analysis uses a causal-inference-informed bifactor model to test if the general factor is stable and unresponsive to domain-specific manipulations, while specific factors change predictably, thereby providing evidence for their distinct functional roles. The final model is validated using multi-method data (behavioral tasks, implicit measures, and informant reports) to ensure the factors represent robust psychological constructs, not just measurement artifacts.

ideas.title: Stable Covariates as Moderators of Teacher Perception Bias in Reciprocal Student-Teacher Belief Models
ideas.description: This research investigates how teachers' potentially biased perceptions of student self-concept and academic efficacy reciprocally influence actual student beliefs over time, while explicitly testing whether stable student covariates like IQ or socioeconomic status moderate these dynamic pathways. The study employs a longitudinal cross-lagged panel model but relaxes the typical assumption of homogeneous effects, allowing the strength of the reciprocal perception-reality links to vary as a function of these covariates. This approach uniquely reveals not just if perception fallacies exist, but for whom they are most potent and self-reinforcing, thereby identifying specific subgroups where interventions to correct teacher perceptions could be most impactful.

ideas.description: This research direction uses intensive longitudinal data on outcomes (e.g., academic performance, task completion) as input to inversely model the evolving network structure between self-concept and self-efficacy items. By applying temporal network psychometrics, it investigates how sequences of successes or failures retroactively alter the strength, direction, and centrality of specific self-belief symptoms, potentially redefining the functional boundary between the constructs. This allows for the identification of critical 'rewiring events' in the self-belief network that precede lasting changes in global self-view, offering a mechanistic, network-level explanation for how outcomes crystallize into stable self-beliefs.

ideas.title: Cross-Domain Subgroup Discovery of Reciprocal Effect Profiles
ideas.description: This research applies machine learning for subgroup detection simultaneously across multiple academic subjects (e.g., mathematics, language arts, science) to identify latent student profiles based on their cross-domain patterns of reciprocal effects between achievement and self-concept. The core innovation is analyzing whether the emergent subgroups are characterized by subject-specific reciprocal effect dynamics (e.g., a 'math-specific virtuous cycle' profile) or by consistent, domain-general patterns (e.g., a 'globally resilient' or 'globally vulnerable' profile). This approach moves beyond asking if effects are 'similar' in different subjects to determining if the fundamental architecture of student motivation and achievement interactions is subject-bound or constitutes a stable learner trait, with significant implications for targeted, holistic educational interventions.

ideas.title: A Dynamic, Multi-Method Investigation of Academic Self-Concept Formation and Influence Across Contexts
ideas.description: This study investigates how general and domain-specific academic self-concepts form and influence behavior by moving beyond static surveys. It employs a longitudinal experience-sampling methodology (ESM) to capture real-time fluctuations in self-concept during academic tasks, complemented by digital trace data for behavioral validation. The design explicitly controls for reference group effects using anchoring vignettes and analyzes how situational factors (e.g., classroom environment, immediate task performance) moderate the relationship between state self-concepts and outcomes like persistence, strategy use, and achievement.

ideas.title: Longitudinal tracking of frame-of-reference effect stability using ecological momentary assessment and life-course analysis
ideas.description: This research adapts longitudinal cohort and life-course analysis methods from epidemiology to investigate the long-term stability and latent emergence of frame-of-reference effects in education. The analogous epidemiological solution involves tracking cohorts over decades with blended assessment strategies (detailed periodic surveys + frequent EMAs) to identify latent health outcomes and critical periods. Adapted to the educational context, the study would follow students for 10-15 years beyond their initial four-year window, using a mixed-methods longitudinal design. This includes traditional surveys at major career milestones combined with smartphone-delivered ecological momentary assessments (EMAs) to sample self-efficacy, attributions, and coping strategies in real-time during challenging professional tasks. The goal is to determine if early framing interventions have sleeper effects, influencing later career adaptability, resilience, or engagement in lifelong learning, which are not detectable in shorter-term studies.

ideas.title: Cross-Domain Validation of Self-Constructs Using Ecological Momentary Assessment
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from Behavioral Medicine—Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA)—to the educational psychology context of the original seed idea. In health psychology, EMA has been successfully used to validate the distinction between general health beliefs and domain-specific self-efficacy by capturing real-time, contextualized data on states and behaviors. The adapted study would employ EMA methodology with students, prompting them multiple times daily to report on their task-specific self-efficacy in language arts and science activities as they naturally occur. This design tests the core distinction between global academic self-concept and situated self-efficacy by examining their dynamic, within-person relationship across different academic domains and real-world contexts, providing stronger ecological validity than traditional cross-sectional surveys.

ideas.title: Test specific intercultural curriculum interventions

ideas.title: Dynamic Network Psychometrics of Self-Beliefs via Ecological Momentary Assessment
ideas.description: This research integrates network psychometrics with ecological momentary assessment (EMA) to study the dynamic architecture of academic self-beliefs. By administering frequent, brief EMA surveys in classroom settings, we collect intensive longitudinal data on specific self-concept and efficacy items. We then apply time-varying network analysis to these data to model the symptom-level interactions as a dynamic system, identifying which momentary beliefs act as real-time catalysts or bottlenecks. This approach reveals how the network structure of self-beliefs shifts in response to specific academic events and predicts near-future emotional and cognitive outcomes.

ideas.title: Investigating the Emergent Properties of Global Academic Self-Concept: An Analogy from Ecosystem Ecology
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from ecosystem ecology's study of emergent system properties, this research investigates if a global academic self-concept relates to efficacy differently than domain-specific ones. In ecology, system-level functions like resilience are not mere sums of species-level traits but arise from interactions. The analogous solution is to measure both component and system levels to model interdependencies. Adapted to educational psychology, the study would measure domain-specific academic self-concepts alongside a distinct global academic self-construct. Using methods like network analysis or hierarchical regression, it would test whether the global construct acts as an emergent property, uniquely predicting overarching academic outcomes such as general efficacy, persistence, and adaptability to novel challenges, beyond the variance explained by the aggregate of domain-specific self-concepts.

ideas.title: Micro-Scale Self-Belief Profiling via Ecological Momentary Assessment
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from ecology's use of fine-grained, continuous environmental monitoring to understand micro-processes within ecosystems, this research adapts that solution to the study of academic self-beliefs. The analogous solution involves deploying sensor networks for real-time, in-situ tracking of specific variables. Adapted to educational psychology, the research idea is to implement Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) to 'scale down' assessments to the micro-level of specific math sub-skills. Students would be prompted multiple times per day via a mobile app to report their self-efficacy and anxiety related to the immediate or very recent micro-task (e.g., 'How confident are you RIGHT NOW about solving this geometry proof?'). This method creates a dynamic, high-resolution 'profile' of self-belief fluctuations across different mathematical contexts, revealing patterns invisible to traditional macro-scale surveys.

ideas.title: Explore domain-specific variations in reciprocal effects

ideas.sourceTitle: The Reciprocal Effects Model is Robust to Alternative Modeling Specifications: A Response to Sorjonen et al., 2025

ideas.title: Modeling Self-Concept and Self-Efficacy as a Bifactor Structure Using Ecological Niche Theory
ideas.description: This idea expands the challenge to the orthogonality assumption between self-concept and self-efficacy by drawing an analogy from ecology. In ecology, the solution to modeling interdependent species traits was the development of the niche hypervolume concept, analyzed using multivariate models that separate a general niche position from specific resource axes. Adapting this solution, the research would employ a bifactor structural equation model in psychology. The model would posit a general 'Global Self-Evaluation' factor underlying all survey items related to academic self-perception, with orthogonal specific factors for self-concept and self-efficacy. This directly tests whether their relationship is best conceptualized as stemming from a common latent core, rather than as distinct, orthogonal constructs, thereby relaxing a key methodological assumption in the field.

ideas.title: Apply network psychometrics to effort dynamics

ideas.title: Cultural Dynamics of Retrospective Self-Concept Formation
ideas.description: This research investigates how cultural contexts shape the process by which sustained success or failure retroactively alters the distinction between self-concept constructs (e.g., self-esteem vs. self-efficacy). It moves beyond a static cross-cultural comparison to model the dynamic, iterative cycle where outcomes influence self-belief distinctions, which then guide future behavior, with the strength and direction of this feedback loop being culturally contingent. The novel synergy lies in testing whether individualistic cultures foster a retrospective refinement of self-beliefs that reinforces personal agency constructs, while collectivist cultures may channel outcomes into more relational or duty-based self-distinctions, thereby offering a unified theory of culturally-mediated self-concept development.

ideas.title: Machine Learning for Predictive Network Discovery in Self-Concept Dynamics
ideas.description: This research direction integrates machine learning for predictive feature discovery with network psychometrics to model the dynamic system of self-concept and achievement. It employs time-series or longitudinal data to construct temporal networks where ML algorithms (e.g., gradient boosting, recurrent neural networks) are used to identify which network features—such as the centrality of a specific self-belief, the strength and direction of edges between beliefs and skills, or the volatility of a node—are the most potent predictors of later efficacy outcomes. The synergy lies in using the discovered predictive network features to iteratively refine and validate a causal, process-oriented model of how self-concept structure influences real-world achievement, offering targets for precise, personalized interventions.

ideas.title: Cohort-sequential longitudinal study with causal modeling to isolate long-term frame-of-reference training effects
ideas.description: This research employs a cohort-sequential design, tracking multiple student cohorts for 10+ years post-intervention, with an active control group that receives an alternative training. It moves beyond simple tracking by collecting annual or biennial data on frame-of-reference stability, domain-specific self-efficacy, career milestones, and key life events. The core analytical innovation is the use of longitudinal causal models to estimate the effect of the initial training while adjusting for time-varying post-graduation experiences. This approach directly tests whether the training exerts a stable, direct effect over time or interacts with specific later experiences to produce emergent self-efficacy, thereby providing robust, attributable evidence for or against long-term psychological impact.

ideas.title: Inverse Agent-Based Modeling of Classroom Dynamics to Infer Latent Self-Belief Trajectories
ideas.description: This research direction combines agent-based modeling (ABM) with an inverse problem approach to study educational psychology. It involves building a detailed computational simulation where agents (students) interact within a virtual classroom, with rules governing how their self-beliefs (e.g., self-efficacy, mindset) evolve from peer comparisons, teacher feedback, and academic results. The core innovation is to then run the model in reverse: using longitudinal, real-world classroom outcome data as a target, the system employs inverse methods and calibration techniques to infer the initial hidden self-belief states and interaction parameters that best explain the observed collective patterns. This allows researchers to test competing theories about how sustained success or failure retrospectively shapes self-concept differentiation by seeing which latent belief dynamics, when simulated forward, reproduce the actual emergent outcomes.

ideas.title: Cognitive Microdosing: Targeted Self-Concept Priming for Immediate Performance Gains
ideas.description: This idea adapts the pharmacological solution of microdosing to the psychological research context. The analogous problem in pharmacology is achieving a targeted, immediate effect with minimal intervention, solved by using precise, ultra-low doses. The adapted research idea is to test 'cognitive microdoses'—extremely brief, targeted interventions designed to prime a specific facet of self-concept. These micro-interventions (e.g., a short affirmation, a success memory recall prompt) would be administered immediately before a participant engages in a specific, time-bound academic achievement test (e.g., a math problem set, a reading comprehension task). The core measurement is the immediate causal effect of this psychological 'microdose' on performance for that subsequent, proximal task, isolating the impact of a boosted self-concept component from broader, long-term training effects.

ideas.title: Theory-constrained causal discovery and experimental validation of self-concept and efficacy predictors
ideas.description: This research system integrates machine learning for causal feature discovery with strong theory-building and experimental validation to ensure robust, interpretable findings. First, it applies constraint-based causal discovery algorithms to longitudinal observational data, but within a pre-registered pool of variables derived from existing psychological theory. The resulting suggested causal structures are formalized into explicit, competing theoretical models. These models are then rigorously tested through preregistered confirmatory analyses in independent datasets and, crucially, through targeted experimental manipulations designed to test the predicted causal pathways between self-concept and efficacy outcomes.

ideas.title: Network-Based Subgroup Detection for Personalized Self-Concept Interventions
ideas.description: This research synthesizes network psychometrics and machine learning-based subgroup detection to move beyond average population models. It uses intensive longitudinal data to construct individual-level networks linking specific self-beliefs (e.g., math self-efficacy, anxiety) and observed skills. Machine learning algorithms (e.g., mixture modeling, community detection on network features) then identify latent subgroups of students who share a common, dynamic pattern of interactions within their self-concept/skill networks. The core innovation is developing and testing subgroup-specific intervention strategies that target the most central or influential nodes within each subgroup's unique network structure to optimally disrupt negative feedback loops or enhance positive synergies between belief and skill.

ideas.title: Dynamic Cognitive Interviewing for Intensive Longitudinal Measurement
ideas.description: This research integrates cognitive interviewing techniques with intensive longitudinal diary methods to investigate the temporal dynamics of item interpretation. By conducting brief, targeted cognitive interviews repeatedly alongside daily surveys of self-concept and effort, the study maps how a student's understanding of scale items fluctuates in relation to daily experiences, stressors, and successes. This approach reveals not only short-term fluctuations in scores but also the underlying shifts in personal meaning that drive those scores, offering a process-oriented validation of longitudinal measures. Consequently, it allows researchers to disentangle true psychological change from measurement artifacts caused by contextual variations in item interpretation.

ideas.title: Apply continuous-time modeling from econometrics

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Succession Models to REM Development Across the Lifespan
ideas.description: This idea challenges the developmental equilibrium assumption in REM analysis by drawing an analogy from ecology's shift away from the 'climax community' paradigm. In ecology, long-term studies and patch dynamics theory revealed continuous, non-equilibrium change. Adapting this solution, the research proposes longitudinal, multi-cohort studies tracking REM development from adolescence through adulthood. It would treat life events and cognitive demands as analogous to ecological disturbances, modeling REM trajectories to understand if, when, and how developmental plateaus, regressions, or progressions occur beyond the previously studied grade 9 period.

ideas.title: Calibrating Perceptual Lenses: A Signal Detection Approach to Teacher Judgments of Student Self-Beliefs
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from clinical psychology, where Signal Detection Theory (SDT) was used to dissect clinicians' often-fallible perceptions of patient symptoms (the 'clinician's illusion'). The analogous problem—systematic bias in professional judgment about internal states—is solved by modeling judgments as comprising two independent components: sensitivity (d') and response bias (c). We adapt this by treating students' self-reported self-concept and efficacy (from reliable instruments) as the true 'signal.' Teachers' perceptions of these student beliefs are then analyzed using SDT metrics. This allows the research to move beyond simple accuracy correlations to determine whether teacher fallacies stem primarily from poor sensitivity (an inability to discriminate between high and low self-concept students) or from a consistent response bias (e.g., a halo effect or systematic underestimation), offering more precise targets for teacher training and support.

ideas.title: Dynamic Self-Belief Construction in a Perceptual Task-Space
ideas.description: This research investigates how mathematical self-beliefs are dynamically constructed in response to specific perceptual and cognitive features of tasks, rather than being fixed to domain labels. Participants solve problems from a modeled 'task-space' where features like symbolic abstraction, diagrammatic reasoning, and procedural fluency are orthogonally manipulated. Real-time measures of self-efficacy, cognitive load, and postdiction accuracy are used to identify which features most powerfully predict self-belief formation and updating. The outcome is a predictive model of self-belief dynamics that can inform targeted, feature-based interventions rather than broad skill-area reassurances.

ideas.title: Disentangling Culture from Construct: A Process-Oriented, Within- and Between-Nation Investigation of Measured Mediators
ideas.description: This research moves beyond comparing nations as cultural proxies by first identifying and quantitatively measuring the specific psychological constructs (e.g., independent/interdependent self-construal, hierarchy preferences) hypothesized to underlie cultural differences. Using stratified sampling across distinct regional and demographic groups within multiple nations (including Germany and several others), the study collects individual-level data on these constructs alongside the primary dependent variables. The analysis explicitly models within-culture variance and tests whether individually measured constructs statistically account for between-group differences, thereby isolating the active ingredients of cultural influence and avoiding the ecological fallacy.

ideas.title: Micro-Task ML for Latent Self-Belief Architecture Discovery
ideas.description: This research applies interpretable machine learning algorithms (e.g., random forests, neural networks) to high-resolution, micro-level educational data. Students provide self-belief ratings and perform tasks across dozens of specific sub-skills (e.g., solving linear equations, proving triangle congruence, interpreting word problems). The ML models are trained not just to predict overall outcomes, but to identify which constellations of micro-skills and micro-beliefs are the most predictive of higher-order constructs like domain-specific self-concept versus efficacy. This reveals the underlying architecture of academic self-beliefs, showing how they are constructed from fundamental, often non-obvious, cognitive and affective components.

ideas.title: Network Analysis of Jingle-Jangle in Applied Self-Perception Constructs
ideas.description: This research applies network psychometrics to the analysis of self-perception constructs in applied settings like sports, performing arts, or creative professions. It uses network analysis to model the item-level interactions between ostensibly similar constructs (e.g., athletic self-concept, performance efficacy, resilience) to empirically identify instances of jingle (same label, different networks) or jangle (different labels, same network). This approach moves beyond theoretical critique to provide a data-driven map of how these self-beliefs are functionally organized and influence each other in practice, offering insights for targeted interventions in coaching, arts education, and professional development.

ideas.title: Experimental Causal Test of Self-Belief Priming on Social Comparison Selection
ideas.description: This research employs a longitudinal experiment to rigorously test whether manipulated changes in academic self-beliefs directly cause shifts in social comparison preferences. Participants are randomly assigned to receive a priming intervention designed to temporarily elevate or lower their domain-specific self-concept, or to a neutral control. Immediately following, they complete a validated behavioral task where they choose between upward, lateral, and downward comparison targets for performance feedback. By measuring comparison choices as the dependent variable after the exogenous manipulation of the independent variable (self-belief), the design establishes temporal precedence and provides stronger causal evidence for the proposed inverted ordering than correlational models.

ideas.title: Apply causal inference methods from economics
ideas.description: Use instrumental variables or regression discontinuity to estimate causal impact of school SES.
